q1.text: Do you think social media has had a positive or negative effect on young people's lives?

q2.text: Some people would argue it actually helps young people stay connected and feel less isolated. What would you say to that?

candidates.name: Sofia
styleCard: Open with a clear opinion phrase like 'In my opinion' or 'I think'
Give one concrete reason to support your view
Acknowledge the other side briefly before restating your position

q1.text: In my opinion, social media has had more of a negative effect on young people. I think the main reason is that it puts a lot of pressure on teenagers to look perfect and get lots of likes. I understand that some people use it to keep in touch with friends, but I really feel that the negatives outweigh the positives when it comes to young people specifically.

alternatives: the drawbacks are greater than the benefits

q2.text: That's a fair point, and I can see why people think that. But I still think online connection isn't the same as real friendship. I know some people say they feel less lonely because of social media, but I'd say most teenagers actually feel more anxious because of it. So on balance, I'd say it does more harm than good.

alternatives: I can see where you're coming from

candidates.name: Luca
styleCard: Get to the point immediately — no long warm-up
Use short, direct sentences to make your argument hit harder
End with a clear, confident conclusion

q1.text: Negative, definitely. Look, it's designed to be addictive — these companies make money from keeping people online as long as possible. Young people don't realise they're being manipulated. I reckon most teenagers would be happier without it, honestly. It creates fake standards that nobody can actually live up to.

q2.text: Yeah, but that's the thing — it's a fake kind of connection. You can have five hundred friends online and still feel completely alone. I'd push back on that idea, because real connection needs real interaction. The studies back this up too — heavy users report higher loneliness, not lower. So that argument doesn't really hold up.

functions.phrase: I'd push back on that idea
that argument doesn't really hold up

candidates.name: Ines
styleCard: Sound natural first, then upgrade one phrase to C1 level
Use a concession move: acknowledge the other view before your own
Finish with a nuanced conclusion, not a simple yes/no

q1.text: It's a complicated question, to be honest. I think there are genuine benefits — staying connected, finding communities, sharing ideas. But what concerns me most is the impact on self-image. There's something quite insidious about a platform that constantly shows you a curated version of other people's lives. On the whole, I'd say the effect has been more damaging than helpful, particularly for younger teenagers.

alternatives: what I find most troubling is
the thing that worries me most is
most notably in the case of

q2.text: I'd acknowledge there's something in that — the connectivity argument has merit. But I'd distinguish between passive scrolling and active communication. The research suggests it's the passive consumption that's harmful, not necessarily messaging friends. So I'd partially agree, but with an important qualification: not all social media use is equal, and the kind that makes young people feel worse is also the most common kind.

functions.phrase: I'd acknowledge there's something in that
alternatives: I'd grant there's merit in that
I'd concede that point has validity

candidates.name: Maya
styleCard: Qualify your position before stating it — avoid sounding absolute
Use a concessive structure: 'While X, nevertheless Y'
Show awareness that the issue is complex before reaching your view

q1.text: It's difficult to offer a straightforward answer, partly because the evidence is genuinely mixed. While there are documented benefits — peer support networks, access to information, creative self-expression — the weight of psychological research does seem to suggest that heavy use correlates with poorer mental health outcomes, particularly among girls. I'd be cautious about making a sweeping generalisation, but if pressed, I'd say the net effect has probably been negative for the majority of young users.

functions.phrase: It's difficult to offer a straightforward answer
alternatives: It would be an oversimplification to say
A simple answer doesn't really do justice to
One can't easily reduce this to a yes or no

functions.phrase: I'd be cautious about making a sweeping generalisation
alternatives: I'd want to avoid an overly broad claim
I'd be wary of drawing too absolute a conclusion
were I to commit to a view
if I had to come down on one side

q2.text: That's a nuanced point and I wouldn't dismiss it. The connectivity argument is well-founded in theory — social support is a recognised protective factor for mental health. However, I'd want to interrogate what we mean by 'connected'. The research makes a fairly consistent distinction between active use — direct messaging, making plans — and passive consumption, which is associated with social comparison and lower wellbeing. So while the argument holds for one type of use, it doesn't generalise to the whole experience.

alternatives: that's not something to be lightly set aside
there's genuine weight to that argument

functions.phrase: I'd want to interrogate what we mean by
alternatives: I'd want to examine the meaning of
I'd challenge what we understand by

candidates.name: Theo
styleCard: Open with an abstract observation about the issue, not your opinion
Use at least one nominalisation — turn a verb into a noun phrase
Build your argument logically, connecting each point with a discourse marker

q1.text: The relationship between social media and adolescent wellbeing is one of the more thoroughly researched areas in contemporary psychology, and the consensus is increasingly clear. The design architecture of these platforms — the variable reward mechanisms, the quantified social validation — is specifically engineered to exploit developmental vulnerabilities in young people. My position is that the harm is structural, not incidental: it's built into the product. The connectivity benefits, while real, are secondary effects that don't compensate for the primary damage to identity formation and attentional capacity.

q2.text: The isolation argument deserves engagement, and I take it seriously. Social connection is a fundamental human need, and to the extent that social media facilitates that, the benefit is real. What I'd contest, however, is the implicit assumption that digital connection is functionally equivalent to in-person interaction. The neurological and psychological literature suggests they activate quite different systems. Furthermore, the platforms that are most heavily marketed to young people prioritise performative self-presentation over genuine connection — which is arguably the opposite of what addresses loneliness.

alternatives: The connectivity case has genuine weight
That argument is worth taking seriously
Where I'd take issue is with

functions.phrase: which is arguably the opposite of
alternatives: which is essentially the reverse of

q1.text: Some people say that students learn better when they study subjects they choose themselves. Do you agree?

q2.text: But don't students also need to study subjects they might not choose, like mathematics or a national language?

candidates.name: Sofia
styleCard: State your position clearly in your first sentence
Support it with a reason introduced by 'because' or 'the reason is'
Use a personal example to make the argument concrete

q1.text: Yes, I agree with that. The reason I think so is that motivation is really the key to learning. When you choose a subject yourself, you actually want to understand it, not just pass the exam. For example, I always did much better in the subjects I was interested in. I think that's evidence that personal interest makes a real difference to how much effort you put in.

functions.phrase: The reason I think so is that
alternatives: The basis for my view is that
To give you a concrete example

q2.text: That's true, and I do think some core subjects are important for everyone. My point is that there should be a balance — students need the basics, but they should also have some freedom. If everything is decided for them, they can lose motivation completely. So I'd argue that a mix of compulsory and optional subjects works best in practice.

alternatives: The key claim I'm making is that
This leads me to conclude that

candidates.name: Luca
styleCard: Lead with your conclusion, then give the reasons
Use rhetorical questions to make your argument more forceful
Keep your justification tight — one strong reason is better than three weak ones

q1.text: Absolutely. Think about it — why would you work hard on something you hate? You wouldn't. Interest drives effort, and effort drives results. It's that simple. The whole idea of forcing students through subjects they don't care about is outdated, frankly. Schools should be helping people find their strengths, not making everyone study the same things.

q2.text: Fine, maths and languages — I'll give you those. There are some basics everyone needs. But the list shouldn't be twenty subjects long. Two or three core things, and then students choose the rest. That way you get the foundations without killing off anyone's interest in learning. The current system is too rigid and it shows — look at how many kids disengage.

candidates.name: Ines
styleCard: Give your answer, then support it with both a reason and an example
Use 'in the sense that' or 'in that' to add precision to your justification
Show you've considered the complexity before landing on your view

q1.text: I'd broadly agree, in the sense that intrinsic motivation genuinely does seem to produce better outcomes. There's a difference between studying to pass and studying to understand — and that difference shows in how much you retain afterwards. That said, I think choice alone isn't enough; it has to be paired with good teaching. But yes, ownership over your learning makes a significant difference to engagement and depth.

alternatives: having a stake in what you study
being invested in your own education

q2.text: Yes, and that's a necessary qualification to what I was saying. Some knowledge is genuinely foundational — it creates the framework everything else hangs on. The argument for compulsory subjects isn't about control, it's about equipping students with the tools to access other knowledge later. What I'd resist is a system where there's no room for student voice at all. The most effective models I've come across tend to combine a strong core with real flexibility at the edges.

alternatives: that's a fair amendment to my point
What I'd push back against is
Where I'd draw the line is

candidates.name: Maya
styleCard: Qualify your agreement — show that 'yes, but it depends on...'
Use hedging language: 'tends to', 'on the whole', 'in most cases'
Identify the condition under which your argument holds true

q1.text: There's a strong body of evidence to support that view, particularly in the area of motivational psychology. The distinction between intrinsic and extrinsic motivation has been fairly well established — and the research tends to suggest that intrinsically motivated learners demonstrate deeper processing and longer retention. I'd agree with the principle, while noting that it applies more clearly to older students, who have the self-knowledge to make informed choices. For younger children, the relationship between choice and learning is more complicated.

functions.phrase: There's a strong body of evidence to support that view
alternatives: The research supports that position strongly
That view has considerable empirical backing
There are good grounds for that claim
generally points to the conclusion that

q2.text: That's an important counterpoint, and I'd be the first to acknowledge it. The case for certain compulsory subjects rests on the argument that some skills are genuinely prerequisite — not just useful, but necessary for meaningful participation in society. Numeracy and literacy are the obvious examples. What I'd want to resist, however, is the extension of that logic to the entire curriculum. The justification for compulsion weakens considerably as you move away from those foundational domains.

functions.phrase: I'd be the first to acknowledge it
alternatives: I have no difficulty conceding that

functions.phrase: The case rests on the argument that
alternatives: The rationale is grounded in the idea that
The justification derives from the claim that
The logic depends on the view that
The case becomes harder to defend

candidates.name: Theo
styleCard: Frame the issue in abstract terms before giving your view
Use nominalisations: 'the imposition of', 'the correlation between'
Reference evidence or a broader principle to anchor your justification

q1.text: The correlation between student autonomy and academic engagement is well-documented, and the theoretical underpinning is robust. Self-determination theory — one of the more influential frameworks in educational psychology — identifies autonomy as a fundamental psychological need, the fulfilment of which is directly associated with higher-quality learning. On that basis, the proposition holds: the imposition of a rigid, non-negotiable curriculum works against the conditions that produce genuine intellectual development.

alternatives: the framework supporting this is well-established

q2.text: The argument for compulsory core subjects is defensible, but it requires careful scoping. The legitimate case rests on the concept of epistemic prerequisites — knowledge and skills without which further learning is structurally inaccessible. That argument is strongest for foundational literacy and numeracy, and weakens considerably as the subject list expands. What I'd resist is the conflation of 'useful' with 'compulsory': the fact that a subject might benefit some students isn't sufficient justification for mandating it for all.

alternatives: it needs to be precisely defined
it depends heavily on how far you extend it
its application needs to be limited

functions.phrase: What I'd resist is the conflation of
alternatives: What I'd challenge is the equation of
Where I'd push back is on treating
What I'd dispute is the identification of

q1.text: Looking at this photograph of a city with heavy traffic, what do you think the people in the picture might be feeling?

q2.text: What do you think the long-term effects of this kind of traffic congestion might be on a city?

candidates.name: Sofia
styleCard: Signal that you're speculating before you speculate — 'I'd guess', 'I think'
Cover at least two different possibilities, not just one
End with a phrase that shows you know you can't be certain

q1.text: Well, it's hard to say for certain, but I'd guess the people are probably feeling quite frustrated or stressed. Being stuck in traffic is really unpleasant, especially if you have somewhere important to be. I imagine the driver at the front might be worried about being late for work. It could also be that some people have just got used to it and don't feel that bothered anymore — like it's just part of their daily routine.

functions.phrase: it's hard to say for certain
alternatives: it's difficult to know for sure
there's no way to be certain

q2.text: I think the effects could be quite serious over time. Air quality would probably get worse, which might cause more health problems for people living in the city. There might also be economic effects — companies might find it harder to deliver goods. I suppose the government might eventually be forced to invest more in public transport, but that's hard to predict without knowing the political situation.

alternatives: might turn out to be severe

candidates.name: Luca
styleCard: Speculate confidently — use 'probably', 'must be', 'I bet'
Read the image quickly and commit to one strong interpretation
Back up your speculation with a logical reason

q1.text: Frustrated, mostly. Probably furious, actually — look at how dense that traffic is. I bet most of those drivers have been sitting there for at least twenty minutes. The ones on motorbikes are probably feeling a bit smug though. What gets me is the exhaust fumes — breathing that in all day must be really bad for you. I'd say the emotional range is somewhere between bored and absolutely fed up.

q2.text: Long term? The city probably chokes itself. Businesses will start moving out, people will stop wanting to live there, property prices might actually crash in the worst-affected areas. Or the opposite — the government panics and throws money at electric buses and cycle lanes. Either way, it's going to force some kind of change. The status quo just isn't sustainable, realistically.

alternatives: there's a real chance they could
can't continue in the long term
is not a viable long-term position

candidates.name: Ines
styleCard: Use 'would appear to' or 'seem to' to sound more sophisticated when speculating
Offer a psychological or social reading, not just a surface description
Hedge your longer-term speculations more carefully than your immediate ones

q1.text: The people in the foreground would appear to be experiencing a mix of frustration and resignation — which I'd speculate reflects a kind of learned helplessness that urban commuters develop when congestion becomes chronic. The person further back could well be entirely switched off, listening to something, mentally somewhere else entirely. I think there's also something worth noting about how differently pedestrians and drivers seem to experience the same physical space.

q2.text: The consequences are likely to be wide-ranging. Most immediately, air quality is probably going to decline further — with the health implications that follow. There are also likely to be economic effects: reduced efficiency, higher logistics costs, and a possible long-term exodus of businesses from the centre. Whether cities respond proactively or reactively is harder to predict — that presumably depends on the political will available, which varies enormously. I'd say the outcome is genuinely uncertain, but the pressure to act will only increase.

alternatives: are probably going to be substantial
seem likely to extend across many areas

functions.phrase: There are also likely to be
alternatives: It's probable there would also be
is more difficult to determine with confidence
would in all likelihood hinge on
would almost certainly be a function of

candidates.name: Maya
styleCard: Distinguish between what you can observe and what you can only infer
Use 'suggests' and 'implies' when drawing psychological conclusions

q1.text: It's impossible to say with any certainty, of course — we can only infer from what's visible. That said, the body language of the driver nearest the camera suggests something closer to resignation than acute frustration, which is an interesting distinction. Prolonged exposure to traffic tends to shift people from active stress to passive disengagement. The pedestrians, if any are visible, might tell a different story — they're at least moving, which changes the psychological experience considerably.

functions.phrase: It's impossible to say with any certainty
alternatives: There's no way to be sure
all we can go on is

q2.text: The long-term effects are likely to be systemic. One could reasonably predict a progressive deterioration in air quality, with attendant implications for respiratory health across the population. There are likely to be economic ramifications too — reduced productivity, supply chain disruption, and potentially the gradual hollowing-out of commercial centres as businesses relocate. Whether this triggers a policy response is harder to forecast: that would presumably depend on the severity of the crisis and the political calculus at the time.

candidates.name: Theo
styleCard: Frame your speculation in systemic or structural terms
Use abstract nouns: 'the psychological impact', 'the cumulative effect'
Distinguish short-term from long-term effects explicitly

q1.text: The immediate phenomenology is difficult to assess from a static image, but one could venture that the predominant affective state is something between habituated frustration and cognitive disengagement. Chronic exposure to high-stimulation, low-control environments — which is essentially what urban traffic congestion represents — tends to produce precisely that dissociative response. The capacity for acute frustration diminishes over time; what remains is a kind of ambient stress that people stop consciously registering.

alternatives: it seems reasonable to speculate that

q2.text: The long-term systemic effects operate on several levels simultaneously. The environmental and public health implications are relatively well-modelled: progressive air quality degradation with associated morbidity increases. The economic effects are more variable and context-dependent — congestion costs are substantial but their distribution across different sectors is uneven. What is harder to predict with confidence is the behavioural response: whether populations adapt, relocate, or exert sufficient political pressure to drive infrastructure change. History suggests the latter is the least likely outcome in the short-to-medium term.

alternatives: unfold across different domains at once

functions.phrase: What is harder to predict with confidence is
alternatives: What is less easy to forecast with certainty is

q1.text: Here are two photographs — one showing someone working from home, and another showing an office environment. Can you compare them and say which situation you think is more productive?

candidates.name: Sofia
styleCard: Name both photos explicitly before comparing

q1.text: In both photos the people are working, but the situations are quite different. In the first one, the person is alone at home, which looks calm and quiet, whereas in the second photo, there are lots of people around, which seems busier and more energetic. In terms of productivity, I'd say it really depends on the type of work — some tasks need focus, others need collaboration.

q2.text: Personally, I find I'm more productive at home, mainly because there are fewer interruptions. Unlike in an office, where people can come and talk to you at any time, at home I can really focus. One difference I've noticed is that I miss the social side, though — working from home can feel quite lonely after a while. So both have their advantages, but for concentration, home wins.

q2.text: Home, easily. I need silence for anything that requires thinking. In an office I'm constantly half-distracted — someone's always talking nearby or pulling you into a meeting. q1.text: The contrast is quite striking. The home worker has a controlled, self-directed environment — quieter, more personal. The office, by contrast, is characterised by density and social energy, which can either fuel creativity or fragment concentration depending on the task. Neither is objectively superior; they serve different cognitive purposes. If I had to choose which is more productive in absolute terms, I'd probably say it depends on the nature of the work — creative collaboration benefits from proximity, whereas deep analytical work doesn't.

alternatives: The two environments are notably distinct
There's a sharp contrast between them

q2.text: My experience aligns with the distinction I just made. For anything requiring sustained concentration — writing, complex analysis — I find home significantly more effective. The office, by comparison, offers a social infrastructure that's genuinely valuable for planning and brainstorming, but comes at the cost of uninterrupted focus time. If I were to draw the distinction that matters most to me, it would be this: home is where I do my best individual thinking; the office is where I do my best collaborative thinking.

alternatives: The office, on the other hand

functions.phrase: If I were to draw the distinction
alternatives: What sets them apart most clearly

candidates.name: Maya
styleCard: Avoid a simple either/or — show the conditions under which each is better
Use 'tends to' and 'is more conducive to' for sophisticated comparison

q1.text: The two environments are optimised for quite different cognitive tasks, which complicates any simple productivity comparison. The home environment tends to be more conducive to what cognitive scientists call deep work — tasks requiring uninterrupted concentration and sustained attention. The office, by contrast, is better suited to collaborative and communicative tasks that benefit from physical proximity and spontaneous exchange. Framing the question as which is more productive overlooks this distinction.

functions.phrase: tends to be more conducive to

q2.text: My personal experience broadly confirms what the research suggests. I find concentrated, analytical work significantly more sustainable from home, where I have control over my environment and can eliminate interruptions. The office, while socially energising, introduces a kind of cognitive taxation that I find particularly costly for writing and complex problem-solving. That said, I'm aware that this isn't universal — some people genuinely work better with ambient social energy around them. The comparison is ultimately individual.

q1.text: The contrast between the two photographs maps fairly directly onto a well-established distinction in organisational psychology: the difference between environments optimised for individual cognitive performance and those optimised for social coordination. The remote setting, characterised by low interruption and high autonomy, aligns with the conditions identified in deep work research as necessary for high-quality cognitive output. The open office, conversely, sacrifices individual performance capacity for collaborative accessibility — a trade-off that is rational for some workflows and destructive for others.

q2.text: My personal experience is consistent with the theoretical framework. For cognitively demanding, solitary tasks — writing, analysis, structured thinking — the home environment is substantially more effective. The office environment, by comparison, imposes an attentional overhead that I find disproportionately costly for that type of work. The calculus shifts for collaborative tasks: the friction cost of remote communication — the delay, the loss of non-verbal cues — means that for genuinely interdependent work, physical co-location retains a meaningful advantage.

candidates.name: Sofia

functions.phrase: I think it's fair to say

styleCard: Disagree directly and without excessive hedging
Use 'I'd challenge that' or 'that's not right'
Make your counterargument in one punchy sentence

q1.text: No — I don't think that's the government's job. Art is a product. If people want it, they'll pay for it. If they won't pay, maybe the market is telling you something. Why should a nurse's taxes fund an opera that she'll never attend and can't afford to attend even if she wanted to? It doesn't make sense to me.

functions.phrase: I don't think that's the government's job
alternatives: that's not something governments should be doing
I'd question whether that's the state's role
I'd resist the idea that government is responsible for this

q2.text: If they can't survive without a subsidy, then maybe they shouldn't survive. That sounds harsh, but it's honest. There are plenty of arts organisations that fund themselves — music festivals, independent theatres, galleries. The ones that struggle are often the most elitist and the least connected to ordinary people. I'd challenge the assumption that 'cultural institution' automatically means 'deserving of public money'.

alternatives: I'd push back against the notion that

candidates.name: Ines
styleCard: Reframe the question before disagreeing — challenge the premise
Use 'I'd take issue with' or 'I'd resist' to vary your disagreement phrases
Propose an alternative framing, not just a rejection

q1.text: I'd actually push back on the framing of that a little. The question assumes that government funding is the natural default for arts support, but that's a culturally specific assumption — in many countries arts are funded primarily through philanthropy or mixed models. My concern with blanket government arts funding is that it tends to favour established, prestigious institutions over newer, more diverse forms of cultural expression. I'd resist the idea that this is the only or best solution.

functions.phrase: I'd actually push back on the framing
alternatives: I'd take issue with how the question is set up
I'd challenge the assumption behind this
I'd resist the terms of the question
I'd push back against the notion that

q2.text: It's a real concern and I wouldn't dismiss it. The collapse of major institutions would represent a genuine cultural loss. But I'd dispute the premise that government funding is the only viable mechanism. What I'd advocate is a mixed model — targeted tax incentives for cultural philanthropy, matched-funding schemes, a stronger expectation of corporate responsibility. The real question isn't whether to fund the arts, but how to do so without entrenching the elitist patterns that already exist.

alternatives: I recognise the weight of that
I'd take issue with the idea that

candidates.name: Maya
styleCard: Disagree carefully — show you've considered the strongest version of the opposing view
Use 'I'd want to interrogate' or 'I'd question whether'
Your disagreement should feel like intellectual resistance, not emotional rejection

q1.text: My position is more nuanced than a simple yes or no. I'd want to interrogate what we mean by government arts funding — who benefits, what gets funded, by what criteria. The empirical picture suggests that public arts subsidy disproportionately flows to established, high-culture institutions serving relatively affluent audiences. On those grounds, I'd question whether current patterns of arts funding are defensible, even if the principle of some public investment in culture is sound.

functions.phrase: even if the principle is sound
alternatives: even where the broad argument holds

q2.text: The survival argument has real force, and I'd acknowledge that the collapse of major institutions would be a genuine loss. But I'd dispute the implicit conclusion that government subsidy is therefore necessary. The survival of institutions is not intrinsically valuable — what matters is whether they serve a broad cultural function. I'd push back on any argument that conflates institutional survival with cultural vitality. Many of the most vibrant cultural forms have emerged precisely outside the publicly funded mainstream.

functions.phrase: The survival argument has real force
alternatives: The case for survival has merit
That argument is not without substance
I'd contest what that seems to imply
I'd push back against the conclusion that

alternatives: I'd resist any position that equates
I'd challenge any view that treats as equivalent
I'd dispute any claim that identifies

candidates.name: Theo
styleCard: Lead with a structural or systemic critique, not a personal one
Use 'the premise is flawed' or 'the argument doesn't hold'
Demonstrate that your disagreement is principled, not arbitrary

q1.text: I'd actually push back on the framing quite significantly. The arts-funding debate is typically presented as a binary — state subsidy versus market abandonment — but this obscures the real issue. My objection to unqualified government arts funding is democratic and distributional: it tends to privilege culturally established, socially exclusive institutions at the expense of forms of cultural expression that are more representative of actual population diversity. The argument for funding needs to be disaggregated — some institutions deserve support; others represent a transfer of resources to already-privileged groups.

functions.phrase: I'd actually push back on the framing
alternatives: I'd challenge the way the question is set up
I'd take issue with the terms of the debate
I'd resist how the issue has been framed

functions.phrase: My objection is democratic and distributional
alternatives: My concern is structural and political
My critique operates at the level of principle
My resistance is grounded in issues of equity
has to be broken down into its components
can't be treated as a single question

q2.text: The institutional survival argument is emotionally compelling but logically problematic. It conflates two separate questions: whether cultural goods have public value, and whether the specific institutions that currently exist are the best vehicles for delivering that value. I'd dispute both assumptions embedded in the survival argument: first, that existing institutions are irreplaceable; second, that public subsidy is the appropriate mechanism even if they are. A more defensible position would examine what cultural outcomes we actually want and then design funding mechanisms accordingly.

alternatives: intuitively appealing but flawed on examination
persuasive at first glance but questionable on reflection
I'd push back on both propositions
The more intellectually honest position would

q1.text: A local council wants to develop their town to attract more tourists. Looking at these options — an arts centre, a food festival, improved transport, a heritage trail, and a hotel complex — which would be most effective?

q2.text: The council has a limited budget. Which single option would you recommend as the top priority?

candidates.name: Sofia
styleCard: Signal your recommendation clearly: 'I'd suggest', 'I'd recommend'
Give a reason for each option you mention
End with a clear priority

q1.text: I think transport links might be the most important starting point. My reasoning is that if people can't get there easily, none of the other options really matter. That said, I'd also suggest the food festival as a good short-term option — it's relatively cheap to organise and could attract visitors quickly. The arts centre is probably a longer-term investment, but it could really put the town on the map.

alternatives: The reason I say that is

q2.text: If I had to choose just one, I'd go for the food festival. It doesn't need a big investment, it can happen quickly, and it creates an experience people want to come back for. Compared to building a hotel or arts centre, the festival is much lower risk. The downside is that it's seasonal, but I think that's worth accepting as a first step.

functions.phrase: If I had to choose just one
alternatives: Were I to pick a single option
If I'm narrowing it to one

candidates.name: Luca
styleCard: Make your recommendation immediately and back it up
Use 'the best bet is' or 'the smart move is'
Be decisive — avoid presenting too many options

q1.text: Transport, no question. Every other option is useless if nobody can get there. Fix the access problem first. After that, the food festival — cheapest, fastest, most flexible. Hotels and arts centres are multi-year projects that might not pay off. The heritage trail is free to walk but costs money to maintain. Food festival wins on every practical metric.

alternatives: is the strongest option by every measure
comes out ahead on all practical criteria
is superior in every relevant respect

q2.text: Food festival. Here's the simple version: low cost, quick to organise, generates buzz, repeatable, scalable. If it fails, you've lost very little. If it succeeds, you've got the evidence and the momentum to push for something bigger. That's how you manage risk on a limited budget. The other options all require committing large sums before you know if there's actually demand.

alternatives: That's the sensible approach to risk management
That's the right way to handle uncertainty
That's good strategic thinking under constraint

candidates.name: Ines
styleCard: Evaluate against a clear criterion before recommending
Use 'from a ... perspective' to organise your evaluation
Show trade-offs rather than simple winners

q1.text: I'd approach this by thinking about return on investment and timeline. Improved transport is, I'd argue, the highest-leverage intervention — without it, every other option is constrained. From a short-term impact perspective, the food festival makes the strongest case: it's scalable, cost-effective, and generates the kind of experiential memory that drives repeat visits. The heritage trail is worth considering too — minimal infrastructure, but with potential to position the town as culturally distinctive.

functions.phrase: I'd approach this by thinking about
alternatives: The way I'd evaluate this is through
I'd assess this against the criterion of
the move with the greatest multiplier effect

q2.text: Given the budget constraint, I'd recommend the food festival — and here's the strategic rationale. Unlike the transport or hotel options, which require substantial capital with a longer return horizon, the festival can be piloted at modest cost and scaled based on demonstrated demand. It also functions as a market-testing mechanism: success provides the evidence base for securing further funding. The risk profile is considerably more favourable and the timeline to measurable impact is much shorter.

alternatives: and the reasoning runs as follows
As opposed to the other choices

candidates.name: Maya
styleCard: Show that the right answer depends on which criteria you prioritise
Use 'in the first instance' or 'in the longer term' to structure your evaluation
Acknowledge the trade-offs in your recommendation

q1.text: The most effective option is likely to depend on how you weight different objectives — immediate visitor numbers, long-term destination identity, economic multiplier effects. If the priority is quick impact on visitor numbers, the food festival probably has the edge. If the ambition is more about long-term destination positioning, the arts centre or heritage trail might be more transformative, even if slower to deliver. I'd be wary of recommending any single option without knowing more about the town's existing strengths.

functions.phrase: is likely to depend on how you weight
alternatives: will hinge on which priorities you emphasise
varies according to the criteria you apply
would most likely come out ahead

q2.text: Subject to the caveat that the best option really does depend on the town's specific context, I'd lean towards the food festival as the priority in a constrained budget scenario. The argument rests on risk-adjusted return: it requires low capital commitment, can generate measurable outcomes relatively quickly, and — crucially — a successful festival creates the evidence base that makes future investment in more capital-intensive options easier to secure. The downside is seasonality, which is real but manageable.

candidates.name: Theo
styleCard: Frame your evaluation using a decision-making principle or framework
Distinguish between first-order and second-order effects
Show that your recommendation follows logically from your criteria

q1.text: I'd evaluate these options through the lens of strategic sequencing and infrastructure dependency. Improved transport is, unambiguously, a first-order investment: without it, demand for every other amenity is structurally constrained. The food festival occupies an interesting second tier — high marginal impact per unit of investment, low sunk cost risk, and the potential to generate network effects through social media exposure. The arts centre and hotel complex are both longer-horizon investments whose returns are highly sensitive to whether the access problem has first been solved.

alternatives: dependent on the prior question of whether

q2.text: Given the budget constraint, the food festival is the rational priority — not because it's the most ambitious option, but because it optimises across the most relevant criteria simultaneously: low capital requirement, short return horizon, scalability, and reversibility. That last criterion is often undervalued: the festival can be discontinued without sunk cost if it underperforms. The other options represent irreversible commitments of capital whose returns depend on conditions that can't yet be verified. Under uncertainty, optionality is valuable.

functions.phrase: optimises across the most relevant criteria simultaneously
alternatives: scores well on all the key dimensions at once
performs strongly across every relevant metric
maximises value on the criteria that actually matter
when outcomes are uncertain, flexibility matters
in conditions of uncertainty, keeping options open has real value
the ability to change course is an asset when the future is unclear

q1.text: You're discussing what has been the biggest positive change in society over the last fifty years. The options are: gender equality, digital technology, medical advances, environmental awareness, and global travel. Which do you think has had the most impact?

q2.text: You need to reach a decision together. How might you move towards a shared conclusion?

candidates.name: Sofia
styleCard: Invite your partner's view using a direct question
Show you're open to changing your mind
Use 'shall we' or 'what do you think about' to move the discussion forward

q1.text: I'm drawn to medical advances, because improvements in life expectancy have affected everyone regardless of where you live. But I'm curious what you think — do you feel any of the others stand out more? I don't want to just push my own view if there's a stronger case for something else. What would you say?

alternatives: My instinct is to go for
I'd be interested in your perspective
Where do you stand on this?

q2.text: OK, shall we try to narrow it down? I think we both agree that medical advances and gender equality are probably the strongest. So maybe we could decide between those two? I'd be happy to go with either, but if I had to choose I'd lean towards gender equality. But I'm open to being persuaded — what do you think would be the best way to look at it?

functions.phrase: shall we try to narrow it down?
alternatives: how about we focus on the strongest options?
should we try to reach a conclusion?
can we start to move towards a decision?

alternatives: I can see the merits of both

q1.text: Digital technology for me, easily. It's changed everything — how we communicate, work, learn, shop. Nothing else on that list comes close in terms of scale. But I know some people would go for medical advances or gender equality. What's your instinct? Do you think scale of impact matters most, or depth of change?

alternatives: Where do you land on this?
Would you say ... is more important?
Is ... the key factor for you?
How much weight would you give to...?

q2.text: Right, let's decide. We've gone back and forth enough. I'll make a proposal: if you agree that scale of people affected is the main criterion, digital technology wins. If it's about depth of structural change, gender equality probably wins. Pick your criterion and that gives us our answer. What's it going to be?

alternatives: Let me suggest a way forward
Let me put something on the table

q1.text: I'd be inclined to argue for gender equality on the grounds that it represents a structural change in how societies organise power, with knock-on effects across virtually every other domain. But I'm conscious I might be applying a particular value framework here. I'd genuinely want to hear whether you see a stronger case for digital technology or medical advances — both seem like plausible counterarguments and I don't think this is a simple question.

functions.phrase: I'd be inclined to argue for
alternatives: I'd be disposed to advocate for
My inclination would be to support

q2.text: Perhaps the most productive route to a shared conclusion is to agree first on how we're measuring 'biggest impact'. If it's scale of people affected, medical advances may edge ahead. If it's depth of structural change, gender equality has the stronger case. I'd suggest we settle on one criterion rather than trying to weigh incommensurable factors against each other. I'm prepared to defer to your judgement on which feels most defensible — does that seem like a workable approach?

q1.text: My instinct is towards gender equality, on the grounds that it represents the kind of deep structural shift that enables other changes to occur — it's not just a change in itself, it's a precondition for further change. But I hold that view with some tentativeness, and I'd genuinely welcome a challenge to it. The case for medical advances is compelling on purely quantitative grounds, and I'm not certain my preference isn't reflecting a particular political leaning. What's your read on this?

q2.text: I think the most intellectually honest path to a conclusion is to make our evaluative framework explicit rather than talking past each other. We seem to be using different criteria — you may be prioritising scale; I'm giving more weight to structural depth. Neither is objectively correct. I'd suggest we agree on one framework and then apply it consistently. I'm genuinely flexible about which we choose — I'd rather reach a well-reasoned conclusion on shared terms than impose my preferred criterion.

alternatives: be transparent about the criteria we're using
clarify the basis on which we're evaluating
agree on what we're actually measuring

q1.text: My preliminary assessment favours gender equality, on the grounds that it operates as what one might call a structural multiplier — a change that doesn't merely improve one domain but reconfigures the conditions under which progress in all other domains occurs. Digital technology, by contrast, is primarily an amplifier of existing social structures rather than a transformer of them. But I recognise that's a contestable framing, and I'd be interested in your assessment of whether the distinction I'm drawing holds up under scrutiny.

functions.phrase: I'd be interested in your assessment of
alternatives: I'd be curious to hear your take on

functions.phrase: whether the distinction I'm drawing holds up
alternatives: whether that distinction is as clear as I'm suggesting

q2.text: The most rigorous path to a shared conclusion would be to operationalise the key variable: what precisely do we mean by 'biggest positive change'? If we define it as maximum reduction in preventable human suffering, medical advances almost certainly win on quantitative grounds. If we define it as maximum expansion of human agency and self-determination, gender equality makes a strong case. I'd propose we choose one definition and apply it consistently — I'm indifferent between them analytically, so I'd defer to your preference on which frame to use.

functions.phrase: I'd propose we choose one definition
